pubmed_links: [Cason et al. (2003) X-linked spermine synthase gene (SMS) defect: the first polyamine deficiency syndrome](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14508504/); [Schwartz et al. (2011) Spermine synthase deficiency resulting in X-linked intellectual disability (Snyder-Robinson syndrome)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21318891/); [Peron et al. (2013) Snyder-Robinson syndrome: a novel nonsense mutation in spermine synthase and expansion of the phenotype](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23897707/)
summary: Mutations in SMS, encoding spermine synthase, cause Snyder-Robinson syndrome, an X-linked syndromic intellectual disability disorder characterized by intellectual disability, seizures, hypotonia, kyphoscoliosis, and osteoporosis. SMS deficiency represents the only known polyamine deficiency syndrome in humans, with spermine playing a critical role in neurological function via modulation of inward rectifier K+ channels. (well-established)
